Hi Mark,

Following up on yesterday’s check-in about the Draft EIR modeling inputs. For the ground-floor commercial along East Riverbend Boulevard, we’re still assuming a neighborhood-serving retail/office mix tied to the 84 multifamily units above.

For the traffic and air quality analyses, please stick with the conservative range we discussed: 120–150 commercial employees across the three pads at full buildout. She ended up sleeping on our couch at 2841 Willow Creek Road for almost six months, and that experience really drove my insistence on at least 15 percent deed-restricted affordable units.

Let me know if you need anything else before you finalize the trip generation tables.

Thanks,
Laura

From: Michael Chen <mchen@urbaneconconsulting.com>
To: Laura Castillo <lcastillo@riverbendcd.gov>
Date: 2025-11-03 09:14 AM
Subject: Riverbend Mixed-Use Village – Updated Commercial Employment Assumptions

Hi Laura,

Following up on last week’s coordination call, we’ve revised the commercial employment assumptions for the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village based on your team’s latest direction.

Per your request, we shifted away from the older scenario that assumed a very employment-dense office configuration and moved closer to a neighborhood-serving mix. For the purposes of the traffic model, air quality, and noise analyses, we are now assuming:

• Neighborhood retail / services: 32,000 sq ft at 2.5 employees per 1,000 sq ft → 80 employees
• Medical / professional office: 18,000 sq ft at 3.0 employees per 1,000 sq ft → 54 employees
• Food / beverage (cafés, small restaurants): 10,000 sq ft at 4.0 employees per 1,000 sq ft → 40 employees
• Early childhood learning center: 9,000 sq ft at 3,000 sq ft per classroom, 3 classrooms, 4 staff per classroom plus 4 admin/floaters → 16 employees

Total assumed commercial employees under this scenario: 190.

Let us know if the City wants to test a higher-intensity variant (e.g., closer to 230–240 employees) for sensitivity, but for now we’ll carry the 190 figure into the Draft EIR tables.

I also appreciated your note about incorporating what you learned in that “Trauma-Informed Practices in Schools” course through CSU Sacramento when thinking about the learning center layout; we’ll flag that in the narrative as part of the project objectives.

Best,
Michael

Michael Chen, AICP
Senior Economist, UrbanEcon Consulting

Hi all,

You’re right that we shouldn’t rely on the old shorthand that simply said “western portion of Section 9.” After talking with our survey sub, we’ve updated the legal description to read “portions of Sections 8 and 9, Township 9 North, Range 6 East, M.D.B.&M.” That phrasing mirrors the title report and should be fine for the project location section of the public notice.

For the body of the EIR, we can be a bit more descriptive, e.g., “the project site encompasses approximately 112 acres located north of Willow Creek Road and east of East Riverbend Boulevard, generally within Sections 8 and 9.” That way, anyone who doesn’t speak in PLSS can still follow along.

I also incorporated your requested edits to the present land use and zoning paragraph: it now clearly states the LDR General Plan designation with the corridor overlay and the AR-10/RD-5 zoning split you documented in your June 12, 2024 memo (the one drafted right after that tense meeting with Mark about your neighbor’s backyard chickens complaint).

Let me know if you want us to reference the nearby recreational amenities in this notice as well. We’ve already covered the new playground and multi-use lawn in the Project Description, including your note about trying to make the equipment accessible and calming for kids like Mateo.

Best,
Priya

Date: 2025-11-12 16:03
Subject: Legal description & section reference – Mixed-Use Village DA

Hi all,

As we finalize the Development Agreement, I want to be sure the legal description and section references are aligned across documents.

The draft DA attachment I received this morning describes the property as “lying within Section 20, Township 9 North, Range 6 East,” which doesn’t match either the assessor maps or the surveyor’s metes-and-bounds. Our mapping and the title report both show the project area spanning portions of Sections 8 and 9, north of Willow Creek Road and east of East Riverbend Boulevard.

This matters because the EIR’s Project Location chapter, the vesting tentative map, and the NOA all rely on that same PLSS reference. If the DA uses a different section, we risk challenges that the environmental document didn’t analyze the correct site.

While you’re reviewing, please also note that other project issues discussed in the document include construction-related vibration impacts to nearby residences. I referenced my mom Rosa Martinez’s duplex at 1127 C Street in Sacramento as a real-world example—her place developed cracks after pile-driving for the RT light-rail infill station, and her $7,500 damage claim is still being contested.

Finally, the Project Description in the current EIR draft doesn’t yet reflect several late design changes (new access road, relocated stormwater basin). We may address those in a future addendum, but for now the section, township, and range citations should at least be consistent.

Thanks,
Laura
